question: Are the wavelength annotations on the spectrum bar corrected so that Red is labeled approximately 700 nm and Violet approximately 400 nm?
Is the Rayleigh scattering formula updated to show an inverse-fourth-power dependence on wavelength (e.g., I ∝ 1/λ⁴)?
Does the corrected body text accurately state that shorter wavelengths (blue/violet) are scattered more easily than longer wavelengths?
Does the explanation for sunsets correctly state that they appear red/orange because the blue light has been scattered away?
Is the conclusion box corrected to state that blue light is scattered most easily and red light is scattered least easily?
Are the intermediate wavelength values (for orange, yellow, green, etc.) updated to form a logical, monotonic progression between ~400 nm and ~700 nm?
Is the formula on the handout cover (sitting on the pedestal) updated to reflect the correct 1/λ⁴ relationship?
Is the red text styling and positioning preserved for the corrected Rayleigh scattering formula line?
Are the bilingual color labels (e.g., 'Red / Rojo') preserved on the spectrum bar?
Is the visual rainbow gradient of the spectrum bar preserved (colors not swapped), with only the numerical annotations changed?
Is the prism illustration on the left side of the poster completely unchanged?
Are the main title ('Why is the Sky Blue?') and subtitle unchanged?
Are the visitors (man and woman), the wooden pedestal, and the stack of handouts physically unchanged (aside from the text correction on the cover)?
Are the background museum elements (ceiling, lights, other exhibits) completely unchanged?
Are the glossy reflections on the poster surface preserved naturally over the areas where text was modified?
Is the corrected text sharp, legible, and free from blur or pixelation?
Are the edited text areas free from visible artifacts like halos, smearing, or background color mismatches?
Do the font styles and sizes of the corrected text match the original poster design?
Is the overall logical consistency maintained across the poster (spectrum numbers, formula, body text, and conclusion all agree)?
